html:
Jakob Ulfsson, born in the 1430s (possibly in 1439 or around 1440, see below), at Hacksta Manor in the parish of Hacksta, in the district of Trögd, in western Uppland; he died in the spring of 1521 and was buried in Uppsala Cathedral. He served as the Archbishop of Uppsala from 1469 to 1515, making him the head of the ecclesiastical province of Sweden. His 46 years in office made him the longest-serving holder of this position.

Jakob Ulfsson was instrumental in the founding of Uppsala University in 1477 and was also responsible for introducing the art of printing to Sweden in the 1480s.

== Biography ==

Jakob Ulfsson was born in Trögd during the 1430s (the memorial plaque in Uppsala Cathedral states the year 1421, but this is likely incorrect; it's more likely around 1435). He died in the spring of 1521 (Åsbrink and Westman indicate the time as "before midsummer"). According to his own accounts, he was of noble lineage on both his father's and mother's sides, but no evidence can be found to support this.

His coat of arms featured a golden eagle's foot with white feathers on a blue field. This coat of arms can still be seen in several churches in Uppland that were painted by Albertus Pictor, such as the Church of the Holy Trinity in Uppsala. In the church of Yttergrans, there is a portrait of Ulfsson kneeling before his coat of arms with an eagle's claw. Albertus Pictor depicted him wearing a pearl-embroidered mass vestment, which is displayed in the museum of Uppsala Cathedral.

Jakob Ulfsson began studying at the University of Rostock in 1457 and was eventually promoted to magister in Rome.

He later became the archdeacon of Växjö. From 1465 to 1470, he resided at the papal curia in Rome. Pope Paul II appointed him Archbishop of Uppsala, bypassing Tord Pedersson Bonde. On April 15, 1470, Archbishop Simon of Antibar ordained Ulfsson as the Swedish archbishop, on behalf of the pope, succeeding Jöns Bengtsson (Oxenstierna), who had died in 1467. The ordination reportedly took place in the church of the Bridgettine Order in Rome. Upon his return to Sweden, Jakob Ulfsson arranged for the feast day of Saint Birgitta to be celebrated throughout the kingdom. In 1482, Pope Sixtus IV authorized the veneration and canonization of Birgitta's daughter, Katarina.

As archbishop, Jakob Ulfsson was a leading figure in the Swedish Council of State (riksrådet). In the political power struggle, he belonged to those who opposed the Sture family. After the war against Russia in 1495–1497, he demanded the resignation of the Regent Sten Sture the Elder. In response, Sten Sture besieged Ulfsson's castle at Almarestäket in June 1497. The archbishop retaliated by excommunicating Sten Sture. When the Danish fleet arrived in Stockholm in August of the same year, Sten Sture was forced to abandon the siege and resigned as Regent in October.

Jakob Ulfsson was instrumental in the founding of Uppsala University in 1477, and during his tenure as archbishop, the university experienced its first period of flourishing. He was also responsible for introducing the art of printing to Sweden in the 1480s. During his long time as archbishop, Biskops-Arnö often served as a meeting place for political discussions. The different factions of influential figures sought to coordinate their actions with the power of the church and the king. Arnö suited everyone's purposes well, and the archbishop developed considerable diplomatic skills.

After retiring from his position in 1515, he chose to live on Arnö. However, he did not cease his political and diplomatic activities and may have been about to be reinstated after the imprisonment of his successor, Gustav Trolle. After the Battle on the ice of Åsunden in late winter 1520, the castle at Arnö was burned down by peasants who had previously fought under Sten Sture the Younger, during which Jakob Ulfsson was captured.

When he was released, he settled in the Carthusian monastery in Mariefred. He was instrumental in bringing the Carthusians to Sweden, and in 1497, he bequeathed his ancestral estate, Hacksta in Uppland, to the Carthusian monastery in Mariefred.

He spent his final days at this monastery and died there in 1521. According to Andreas Rhyzelius's chronicle of bishops, his "already decaying body" was moved from the monastery in Mariefred in 1526 by Archbishop Johannes Magnus to Uppsala Cathedral, where it was placed "between the altar screen and the cathedral clock."

== The Lineage of Jakob Ulfsson ==

The term "Jakob Ulfsson's lineage" is not a widely accepted or conventional name, but it is sometimes used to refer to Jakob Ulfsson and his sisters: Margareta Ulfsdotter, who married the knight Gjord Petersson (Schack of Skylvalla), and another sister whose name is unknown, who married 1) Erik Johansson (Ulfsax) and 2) Nils Magnusson (Plata).

== Images == Jakob Ulfsson has been depicted in two images: one on a vestment in the Uppsala Cathedral, and another in a fresco by Albertus Pictor in the church of Yttergran, Håbo municipality, Uppsala County. The vestment is an elaborate garment used for festive occasions or ceremonial events. In both images, he is kneeling behind his coat of arms, a motif also found in many other works by Albertus Pictor. The coat of arms with the eagle indicates that these portraits were created during his time as Archbishop. His depiction in the fresco at Yttergran church...

How certain are we about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well-documented in archives. Uncertainty arises more from interpretation and the perspective of later generations – particularly because those in power themselves played a role in shaping historical narratives.
tldr: Jakob Ulfsson was a powerful Archbishop of Uppsala who led the Church in Sweden for an extended period. He played a significant role in Swedish history, as he helped to establish Uppsala University and introduced the printing press.
lattlast:
Jakob Ulfsson lived in the 15th century and was an archbishop, meaning he was the highest leader within the church in Sweden. He held this position for nearly 50 years, which was a very long time for that era. During his tenure as leader, he was a significant figure in the country's politics and made many decisions that influenced how the nation was governed.

He is best known for being instrumental in the founding of Uppsala University in 1477. He was also responsible for introducing printing technology, i.e., the technique of printing books using machines, to Sweden in the 1480s. This marked a significant change in how knowledge was disseminated throughout the country.

Jakob Ulfsson was also a skilled diplomat who frequently met with other leaders to resolve conflicts between different groups within society. He died in 1521 and is now buried in Uppsala Cathedral, which is the city's main cathedral.
